title: Data Leakage: The Silent Killer
concept: Data leakage occurs when information from the test dataset accidentally leaks into the training dataset, causing overly optimistic evaluation metrics.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a 'missing indicator' column track?

options.text: Whether a specific data value was originally blank before imputation
The exact mathematical average of the column
The total number of columns in the dataset

exercises.explain: Right! A missing indicator flags whether a value was blank (using 1 for yes, 0 for no) so the model knows it was imputed.

cards.title: Cheating on the test
cards.body: Imagine a teacher accidentally leaves the answer key on the back of the study guide. Students get 100% on the practice test, but fail the real exam. That is data leakage.

cards.title: Data Leakage
cards.body: Data leakage happens when info from the future (the test set) is mixed into the training set. The model looks highly accurate in testing, but fails in the real world.

cards.title: Looking at the cards
cards.body: Imagine playing poker, but you can see the reflection of your opponent's cards in the window. You play perfectly during the game, but when the curtains are closed, your skills vanish.

cards.title: Leaky Imputation
cards.body: If you calculate the average age of the entire dataset and use it to fill training blanks, you just leaked test data info into training. You must compute averages using ONLY training data!

cards.title: 100% accuracy is a warning
cards.body: Beginners celebrate when their model gets 99.9% accuracy on the first try. Experienced engineers get suspicious. In ML, perfect accuracy almost always means data leakage.

cards.title: Leaky vs Safe Processing
cards.body: How to avoid leaking information during data cleaning:

left.text:
Split data into train/test
Calculate average of ALL data
Fill blanks in both using that average

right.text:
Split data into train/test
Calculate average of TRAIN ONLY
Fill train and test using train average

exercises.prompt: What is data leakage in machine learning?

options.text: When test data information accidentally bleeds into the training process
When a hacker steals your dataset from the server
When the model takes too long to finish training
When the training data is deleted by mistake

exercises.explain: Exactly! Leakage gives the model a sneak peek at the test set, creating false confidence.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If you calculate the imputation mean using the entire dataset before splitting it into train/test sets, you have introduced data leakage.
exercises.explain: True! The imputation mean of the whole dataset contains information about the test set. You must split first, then calculate the mean on training only.

exercises.sentence: To prevent leakage, calculate imputation statistics using only the ___ dataset.
exercises.explain: Yes! Calculating statistics on the training set only keeps the test set completely unseen and clean.

exercises.prompt: Which one is a sign of data leakage?

options.text: A model achieving 99.9% test accuracy on a highly complex real-world problem
A model showing 75% accuracy on both training and test data
A model that performs poorly on both datasets

exercises.explain: Correct! Unusually high or perfect accuracy on a complex task is the primary warning sign of data leakage.

exercises.prompt: You split your data into train and test sets. You calculate the median age of the test set and use it to fill missing ages in the test set. Does this cause data leakage?

options.text: Yes, because test set processing must only use statistics calculated from the training set
No, because you kept the test set and training set split
Yes, because you are not allowed to split the dataset
No, because median imputation never causes leakage

exercises.explain: Amazing job! To truly simulate the real world, the test set must be treated as completely unknown. Any fill value must come from the training set's statistics. You've conquered Unit 6!
